content.text: How Claude Response Time Breaks Down
Claude response latency is not a single number — it is the sum of several distinct phases. Understanding which phase dominates your latency tells you where to invest optimization effort.
The two most important latency metrics in practice are:
: how long before the first output character appears. This is what the user perceives as "responsiveness." In a streaming UI, TTFT is the delay before the response starts appearing.
: TTFT + the time to generate all output tokens. Determined by TTFT plus output token count × tokens-per-second rate.

TTFT = network RTT + input processing time + pre-generation inference time. Total time = TTFT + (output_tokens / tokens_per_second). Streaming lets you start showing output as soon as TTFT completes, hiding the output generation time from the user.

attrs.alt: Claude response time breakdown showing network RTT, input processing, pre-TTFT inference, and token output phases with typical latency values

content.text: Every Claude API call pays a round-trip network cost: the time for your request to reach the server and the first response byte to return. This is determined by physical distance and routing.
colocate your application server and Claude in the same cloud provider region
. Moving from a cross-cloud or cross-continent call to an in-region call routinely saves 100–200ms of network overhead — the single highest-ROI latency optimization available.
Before generating a single output token, Claude reads and processes your entire input. This processing time scales with input length. Rule of thumb:
, though the real figure varies with model size and server load.

import anthropic, time

client = anthropic.Anthropic()

def time_calls_at_different_prompt_lengths():
    models = ["claude-haiku-4-5", "claude-opus-4-8"]
    lengths = [100, 1000, 10000, 50000]

    for model in models:
        print(f"\n{model}")
        for n in lengths:
            prompt = "x " * n  # n tokens (approx; actual tokenization varies)
            start = time.perf_counter()
            response = client.messages.create(
                model=model,
                max_tokens=10,    # minimal output to isolate input processing time
                messages=[{"role": "user", "content": prompt + "\n\nReply: yes"}],
            )
            elapsed = time.perf_counter() - start
            print(f"  {n:6} tokens input → {elapsed:.2f}s TTFT (actual tokens: {response.usage.input_tokens})")

time_calls_at_different_prompt_lengths()
Prompt Caching eliminates repeated input processing for static prefixes. When you use
, the cached portion is processed once (cache write) and then retrieved from cache on subsequent calls (cache read) — the read typically costs ~10% of the processing time versus a cold cache miss.

Output generation time is proportional to the number of tokens generated. Claude generates tokens sequentially — each new token depends on all previous tokens. The generation speed (tokens per second) is largely fixed by the model's architecture and server hardware.
to the minimum needed for your use case — if you only need 100 words, don't set
; (2) use a smaller model for tasks that don't require Opus-level reasoning; (3) stream to improve perceived responsiveness while output is still generating.

Larger models (Opus) have more parameters than smaller ones (Haiku). More parameters means more computation per token, resulting in lower tokens-per-second throughput and higher TTFT. The latency difference between Claude Opus and Claude Haiku can be 3–5x on the same task.
Model selection is the highest-leverage latency lever after network proximity:
: fastest; use for classification, extraction, routing, simple Q&A, and any task where the output is short and correctness is straightforward to verify
: balanced; use for summarization, moderate reasoning, code generation, customer service responses
: slowest; reserve for complex multi-step reasoning, long document analysis, difficult coding tasks, and cases where accuracy outweighs latency
: route simple requests to Haiku and only escalate to Sonnet or Opus when the Haiku response fails a quality check. This reduces average latency without sacrificing quality on hard cases.

), Claude internally reasons through the problem before generating the visible response. Thinking tokens add to TTFT because they are generated before the first visible output token. The budget you set is an upper bound; Claude uses as many thinking tokens as it needs up to that budget.

Use extended thinking only when you need it: multi-step math, complex reasoning, logic puzzles. For straightforward tasks, extended thinking adds TTFT with no quality benefit. Disable it for latency-sensitive applications.

Prompt Caching reduces TTFT by eliminating re-processing of static input content. A cache hit on a 10,000-token system prompt saves approximately 10ms of input processing time (and costs only 10% of the normal input price).

The cache warm-up (first call = cache write) pays the full input processing time. Subsequent calls within the TTL pay 10% of that time. In production, ensure the cache is warm before serving user traffic:

import anthropic

client = anthropic.Anthropic()

SYSTEM_PROMPT = open("large_context.txt").read()  # e.g. 50K tokens

def warm_cache():
    """Call once at service startup to prime the prompt cache."""
    client.messages.create(
        model="claude-opus-4-8",
        max_tokens=1,             # minimal output — we just want the cache write
        system=[{
            "type": "text",
            "text": SYSTEM_PROMPT,
            "cache_control": {"type": "ephemeral"},
        }],
        messages=[{"role": "user", "content": "ping"}],
    )
    print("Cache warmed")

# Call at service startup
warm_cache()

# Subsequent calls are cache-hit — TTFT is much lower
def answer(question: str) -> str:
    response = client.messages.create(
        model="claude-opus-4-8",
        max_tokens=512,
        system=[{
            "type": "text",
            "text": SYSTEM_PROMPT,
            "cache_control": {"type": "ephemeral"},
        }],
        messages=[{"role": "user", "content": question}],
    )
    return response.content[0].text

Do not rely on anecdotal observations — measure latency quantitatively using percentiles. p50 (median) tells you the typical user experience; p95 and p99 tell you the worst-case experience for 5% and 1% of users respectively.

The latency figures above assume your request is served immediately. Under high load, requests may queue behind other requests from your account or from other customers (if using a shared quota pool). Queue time is invisible in your application code — it looks like higher TTFT — but it is not model inference time.

Queue time can be detected by comparing expected TTFT (from your benchmarks) to observed TTFT under load. A sudden TTFT increase without a change in prompt length usually indicates queuing. Mitigations:
: Higher RPM and ITPM limits reduce the likelihood of queuing at the API layer
: Spreads requests across multiple regions, reducing per-region queue pressure
: Buffer requests in your application and send them at a controlled rate, avoiding burst spikes that trigger queuing
: For batch workloads, run during low-traffic hours when API queue depth is lowest

import asyncio, anthropic

# Rate-limited caller: max N concurrent requests at any time
class RateLimitedClaude:
    def __init__(self, max_concurrent=5):
        self.client = anthropic.AsyncAnthropic()
        self.semaphore = asyncio.Semaphore(max_concurrent)

    async def call(self, messages, model="claude-haiku-4-5", max_tokens=512):
        async with self.semaphore:  # at most max_concurrent calls at once
            return await self.client.messages.create(
                model=model,
                max_tokens=max_tokens,
                messages=messages,
            )

async def process_batch(texts):
    caller = RateLimitedClaude(max_concurrent=5)
    tasks = [
        caller.call([{"role": "user", "content": f"Classify: {t}"}])
        for t in texts
    ]
    return await asyncio.gather(*tasks)

# Run
results = asyncio.run(process_batch(["positive text", "negative text", "neutral text"]))
for r in results:
    print(r.content[0].text)

lets you issue multiple Claude requests concurrently, overlapping their latencies. If you need 10 independent summaries, firing them all in parallel takes max(TTFT_i + generation_i) time instead of sum(TTFT_i + generation_i) — potentially 10x faster.

import asyncio, anthropic

async def batch_summarize(documents: list[str]) -> list[str]:
    client = anthropic.AsyncAnthropic()

    async def summarize(doc: str) -> str:
        response = await client.messages.create(
            model="claude-haiku-4-5",
            max_tokens=256,
            messages=[{"role": "user", "content": f"Summarize in 2 sentences:\n\n{doc}"}],
        )
        return response.content[0].text

    # All summaries run concurrently (up to API rate limits)
    results = await asyncio.gather(*[summarize(d) for d in documents])
    return list(results)

docs = ["Document A...", "Document B...", "Document C..."]
summaries = asyncio.run(batch_summarize(docs))
for s in summaries:
    print(s)
) is a drop-in replacement for the sync client — same methods, same return types, all awaitable. Use it for any server-side code that processes multiple requests per event loop tick.

Non-streaming calls buffer the entire response server-side before returning it to you. The connection stays open but no bytes arrive until the model has generated all output tokens. This means your user waits for total response time before seeing anything.

Streaming calls return the response token-by-token as it is generated. The user sees the first token at TTFT and reads along as Claude writes. For a 500-token response, this difference can be 5–15 seconds of perceived wait time eliminated.
Programmatic processing; batch; structured output parsing
User-facing chat; long outputs; any interactive UI

Always stream user-facing calls. Disable streaming only when your downstream code needs the complete response before it can do anything (e.g. JSON parsing, structured output extraction, function dispatch based on full output).
A Service Level Objective (SLO) defines the latency target your application must meet. A reasonable starting point for a Claude-backed chat application:

Measure these SLOs with a continuous synthetic monitor: a cron job that fires a fixed test prompt every minute, records TTFT and total time, and pushes metrics to CloudWatch or Datadog. Alert when any SLO breaches for 5 consecutive minutes — that is the signal to investigate queuing, regional degradation, or network issues.

import anthropic, time, json, os

client = anthropic.Anthropic()

TEST_PROMPT = "Reply with exactly three words: working fine today"

def health_check() -> dict:
    start = time.perf_counter()
    first_token = None
    text = ""

    with client.messages.stream(
        model="claude-haiku-4-5",
        max_tokens=10,
        messages=[{"role": "user", "content": TEST_PROMPT}],
    ) as stream:
        for chunk in stream.text_stream:
            if first_token is None:
                first_token = time.perf_counter() - start
            text += chunk

    total = time.perf_counter() - start
    return {
        "ttft_ms": round((first_token or 0) * 1000),
        "total_ms": round(total * 1000),
        "ok": "working" in text.lower(),
    }

result = health_check()
print(json.dumps(result))
# {"ttft_ms": 423, "total_ms": 621, "ok": true}

Claude response latency is the sum of six factors: network round-trip time, input token processing, pre-TTFT inference, output token generation, model size, and extended thinking overhead. The most impactful optimizations are: (1) colocate app and Claude in the same cloud region to cut network RTT; (2) use the smallest model that meets quality requirements — Haiku is 3–5x faster than Opus; (3) set

tightly; (4) stream responses to minimize perceived latency; (5) use Prompt Caching for large repeated system prompts; (6) disable extended thinking for latency-sensitive paths. Measure TTFT and p95 total latency before and after each optimization to confirm actual gains.
